item.title: The Bristol sessions
item.description: Date: July 25 – August 5, 1927 Location: Taylor-Christian Hat Company building, 408 State Street, Bristol, Tennessee Key figures: Ralph Peer, the Carter Family, Jimmie Rodgers, Ernest Stoneman In the summer of 1927, Ralph Peer of Victor set up a temporary studio on the upper floors of a hat company’...

item.title: Dance
item.description: RYM On Valentine’s Day 1970, David Mancuso needed rent money and threw a party at his loft on 647 Broadway: about a hundred guests, two dollars and fifty cents at the door for food, punch, and a sound system assembled like a hi-fi obsessive’s living room (because it was one), no liquor license beca...

item.title: Motown sound
item.description: Period1959–1972LocationHitsville U.S.A., 2648 West Grand Boulevard, Detroit, MichiganFiguresBerry GordyThe Funk BrothersHolland-Dozier-HollandSmokey RobinsonNorman WhitfieldLinksRYM The “Motown sound” describes the specific acoustic character of records made at Hitsville U.S.A.’s basement studio (t...

item.title: The color line in pop
item.description: The color line in American popular music is not a metaphor.

item.title: The transatlantic feedback loop
item.description: The pattern that defines the transatlantic movement of popular music — Black American musicians creating a form, white British musicians absorbing and re-exporting it, America responding to the British version — has repeated across so many decades that it functions as a permanent circuit.

item.title: The Loft
item.description: Date: February 14, 1970 Location: 647 Broadway, NoHo, Manhattan Key figures: David Mancuso On Valentine’s Day 1970, a twenty-five-year-old audiophile named David Mancuso needed help with the rent and threw a party in his loft at 647 Broadway.
